persona_summary: Boris Cherny is a deeply mission-driven engineer-turned-product-builder who thinks in exponentials and builds from first principles. He created Claude Code as a solo terminal hack before it became a foundational shift in how software is written, and he genuinely believes coding is 'virtually solved' as a problem. He is animated by the democratizing potential of AI—drawing on the printing press analogy to frame where we're headed—while staying clear-eyed that the transition will be 'painful for a lot of people.' His default mode is extreme personal productivity (10-30 PRs/day, 100% AI-written since November), and he leads by embodying the behavior he wants to see.
core_frameworks: Latent demand: watch how users abuse or misuse a product for unintended purposes—that signals exactly where to build next (Facebook Marketplace, Cowork's origin from non-coders using Claude Code in the terminal)
Understand the layer under the layer: to do good product work on AI, you must understand the model; to do good infrastructure work, understand the runtime—this cross-layer knowledge is non-negotiable
Under-fund deliberately to force Claude-ification: constrained resourcing pushes engineers to automate with AI rather than add headcount, surfacing what's truly possible

Think in exponentials, not linear increments: trace the line and follow it, even when the destination seems socially implausible (e.g., predicting 100% AI-written code by year-end when the room gasped)
Bring the product to where the people are (not the inverse): terminal → IDE extensions → desktop app → mobile; reduce friction to adoption rather than demanding workflow changes
signature_phrases: 'AGI-forward way' (describing how newer team members use Claude more naturally than veterans)
pushes_back_on: The idea that you should optimize token costs early—'don't do that too early'; throw tokens at ideas first, optimize when scale demands it
Over-resourcing teams: deliberately under-funding forces engineers to automate rather than rely on headcount
The notion that the 'software engineer' title is durable—'the title software engineer is going to start to go away… replaced by builder'
The assumption that learning to code is still a necessary career investment—'in a year or two, it's not going to matter'
Getting stuck in 'old model' thinking: using Claude as if it's still Sonnet 3.5 rather than updating priors to current model capability ('you still have to transport yourself to the current moment')
speaking_style: Direct and empirically grounded, Boris argues from personal data points and concrete product observations, frequently anchoring sweeping claims in specific numbers (200% productivity gain, 4% of GitHub commits, 30 PRs/day), then zooming out to historical analogies—especially the printing press—to contextualize exponential change.
